title: One thing I love about Deities & Demigods (Dungeons & Dragons)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XIhM9oLObuU

Daniel (@dungeondive) reads a recently-donated 1980 first-print of **Deities & Demigods** — the AD&D supplement co-written by the late James M. Ward — and singles out the section he genuinely loves: the Cthulhu Mythos chapter.

The thing that makes the chapter remarkable is the *time it sits in*. In 1980, Lovecraft scholarship was hard. There was no Wikipedia. No Cthulhu Pedia. The original stories were only available through Arkham House mail-order. A teenage gamer who wanted Mythos for their D&D game had almost nowhere to find consolidated lore. This book put it all in one place — entity by entity, with stats. Cthulhu as a greater god, AC -2, 400 HP, 30 attacks, regenerating 10 HP per round, *teleporting half a mile at will, totally immune to the effects of water*. Azathoth at the centre of the universe. Hastur, Cthugga, the Mi-Go, Nyarlathotep with his many masks, the shoggoths created and abandoned by the primordial ones, Yog-Sothoth as the key and the guardian of the gate. Ithaqua gets pride of place in Daniel's reading, on the strength of Brian Lumley's *Born of the Winds*.

Daniel adds a personal aside: he wasn't allowed to play D&D as a child, his household swept up in the early-1980s satanic panic. The donation of this book to the channel is, in a quiet way, a restored permission slip. He also flags the famous TSR / Arkham House / Chaosium copyright kerfuffle that left this first printing un-attributed.

----

Daniel reads Deities & Demigods now partly as a *historical document* — a 1980 attempt to put Mythos lore in one place before the internet existed. Do you read old RPG supplements differently knowing they were once the *only* source for their material?
